text: For a long while there was complete silence among those within; and at last it was broken by Clym, who said in an agonized voice, “O doctor, what does it mean?”
source_text: For a long time there was utter silence among the group within; and it was broken at last by Clym saying, in an agonized voice, “O Doctor, what does it mean?”

text: “Ay, assuredly, it concerns a witch, and you must excuse my hour; for, said I, ‘I must go and tell them, though they will not have half finished dinner.’ I assure you it set me trembling like a driven leaf. Do you think any harm will come of it?”
source_text: “Ay, sure, about a witch, and ye must overlook my time o’ day; for, says I, ‘I must go and tell ’em, though they won’t have half done dinner.’ I assure ye it made me shake like a driven leaf. Do ye think any harm will come o’t?”

text: The triumph of my enemy grew with the hardship of my labors. One inscription he had left ran thus: “Prepare! Your toils have but just begun; clothe yourself in furs and make ready provisions, for we shall presently set forth on a journey wherein your sufferings shall appease my eternal hatred.”
source_text: The triumph of my enemy increased with the difficulty of my labours. One inscription that he left was in these words: “Prepare! Your toils only begin; wrap yourself in furs and provide food, for we shall soon enter upon a journey where your sufferings will satisfy my everlasting hatred.”

text: “Do not believe that,” said Fagin. “When a man proves his own enemy, it is only because he has been too much his own friend; not because he is considerate of all the world except himself. Pooh! pooh! There is no such thing in nature.”
source_text: “Don’t believe that,” said Fagin. “When a man’s his own enemy, it’s only because he’s too much his own friend; not because he’s careful for everybody but himself. Pooh! pooh! There ain’t such a thing in nature.”

text: Within two minutes I was kneeling beside the child’s crib, while Sandy was sending servants hither and thither, all through the palace. I grasped the situation almost at a glance—membranous croup! I bent low and whispered:
source_text: In two minutes I was kneeling by the child's crib, and Sandy was dispatching servants here, there, and everywhere, all over the palace. I took in the situation almost at a glance--membranous croup! I bent down and whispered:

text: “I thought I knew the man’s face when I saw him on the rick—I thought I did! And where are ye trading it to now, shepherd?”
source_text: “Thought I knowed the man’s face as I seed him on the rick!—thought I did! And where be ye trading o’t to now, shepherd?”

text: “A very good thing,” he remarked. “But suppose—and we must suppose such things at times, my dear—suppose he excites her interest in himself too especially?”
source_text: “A very good thing,” he commented. “But suppose,—and we must suppose, sometimes, my dear,—suppose he arouses her interest too particularly in him?”

text: “Now see here, Joe,” he said; “if you go on like that, I’ll strike you over the head. And for the sake of old friendship I’ll strike it hard. Savve?—you will, will you?”
source_text: “Now look here, Joe,” he said; “if you act that way, I’ll punch your head. And for old friends’ sake I’ll punch it hard. Savve?—you will, will you?”

text: “No, no—it is nothing of the kind!” he said, with impatience. “Five years, nine months, and a few days. Nearly fifteen months have gone by since he disappeared, and is there anything so astonishing in an engagement of little more than five years?”
source_text: “No, no—it’s no such thing!” he said, with impatience. “Five years, nine months, and a few days. Fifteen months nearly have passed since he vanished, and is there anything so wonderful in an engagement of little more than five years?”

text: “I do not wish to hear of him; I have heard quite enough of him,” said the stranger, cutting short Mr. Bumble just as he was launching into a torrent upon poor Oliver’s vices. “I mean the woman—the old crone who attended his mother. Where is she?”
source_text: “It’s not of him I want to hear; I’ve heard enough of him,” said the stranger, stopping Mr. Bumble in the outset of a tirade on the subject of poor Oliver’s vices. “It’s of a woman; the hag that nursed his mother. Where is she?”

text: “That is because you, too, have grown countrified. Oh, I could never live in a street for anything in the world! Egdon is an absurd old place; yet I have become accustomed to it, and I could not be happy anywhere else whatever.”
source_text: “That’s because you have got countrified too. O, I couldn’t live in a street for the world! Egdon is a ridiculous old place; but I have got used to it, and I couldn’t be happy anywhere else at all.”

text: “Oh, I remember—the time when the Church first put forth her strength against us, and soon deemed it wise to wait for a more hopeful season. Well, how have you contrived the fence?”
source_text: “Oh, I remember--the time the Church tried her strength against us the first time, and presently thought it wise to wait for a hopefuler season. Well, how have you arranged the fence?”

text: “Now,” he said, coming back—so it seemed from the sound—to the place where he had stood before, “you have given us most valuable assistance, young woman, and I wish you to profit by it. What can I do to serve you?”
source_text: “Now,” he said, returning: so it seemed by the sound: to the spot where he had stood before, “you have given us most valuable assistance, young woman, and I wish you to be the better for it. What can I do to serve you?”

text: “There, begone! Obey her. I shall be your ruin. It is foolish in you to come to me thus. Kiss me, and be gone forever. Forever—do you hear?—forever!”
source_text: “There, go away! Obey her. I shall ruin you. It is foolish of you to meet me like this. Kiss me, and go away forever. Forever—do you hear?—forever!”

text: “You mustn’t tell anyone you saw me, Dick,” said Oliver. “I am running away. They beat me and misuse me, Dick; and I am going to seek my fortune somewhere far away, I know not where. How pale you are!”
source_text: “You musn’t say you saw me, Dick,” said Oliver. “I am running away. They beat and ill-use me, Dick; and I am going to seek my fortune, some long way off. I don’t know where. How pale you are!”

text: “There—Mrs. Coggan is going!” said Bathsheba, letting out her relief in a long breath that had been held within her bosom for a minute or more.
source_text: “There—Mrs. Coggan is going!” said Bathsheba, exhaling her relief in the form of a long breath which had lain in her bosom a minute or more.

text: “Concerning her—no! I believe she has but just returned home with her husband. They made their arrangements to come back this afternoon—to the inn farther on from here.”
source_text: “About her—no! She has just come home, I believe, with her husband. They arranged to return this afternoon—to the inn beyond here.”

text: “Yes, I know it; and I have no sword with me here; but I think I could obtain one by evening. Now, will you do this?”
source_text: “Yes, I know; and I have no sword here; but I think I could get one by the evening. Now, will you do this?”

text: “How am I to find her?” said the stranger, taken off his guard; and he plainly showed that all his fears, whatever they might have been, were stirred anew by the intelligence.
source_text: “How can I find her?” said the stranger, thrown off his guard; and plainly showing that all his fears (whatever they were) were aroused afresh by the intelligence.

text: “No stranger. Sergeant Troy. I am staying in this place.—There! At last it is undone, you see. Your nimble fingers were quicker than mine. I wish it had been the knot of knots, the one that cannot be untied!”
source_text: “No stranger. Sergeant Troy. I am staying in this place.—There! it is undone at last, you see. Your light fingers were more eager than mine. I wish it had been the knot of knots, which there’s no untying!”

text: “Which ones? Indeed, they had no regard for particulars of that sort. They condemned them all together. Know ye not the law?—that, as men say, the Romans left behind them here when they departed—that if one slave slay his master, all that man’s slaves must die for it.”
source_text: “_Which_ ones? Indeed, they considered not particulars like to that. They condemned them in a body. Wit ye not the law?--which men say the Romans left behind them here when they went--that if one slave killeth his master all the slaves of that man must die for it.”

text: The most unhappy of women is awed by a man of sorrow; and though Boldwood, in heat and fervour, was almost her own nature wrought into another sex, Bathsheba’s cheek trembled. She drew her breath hard, and cried, “Leave me, sir—leave me! I am nothing to you. Let me go on!”
source_text: The most tragic woman is cowed by a tragic man, and although Boldwood was, in vehemence and glow, nearly her own self rendered into another sex, Bathsheba’s cheek quivered. She gasped, “Leave me, sir—leave me! I am nothing to you. Let me go on!”

text: “Hold your noise,” cried Sikes, as the dog withdrew beneath the bed, still snarling in fury. “What have you got to say for yourself, you shriveled old fence, eh?”
source_text: “Hold your din,” cried Sikes, as the dog retreated under the bed: still growling angrily. “What have you got to say for yourself, you withered old fence, eh?”

text: “Well, Brother Snell,” said Mr. Noyes—they were both members of a secret organization, in which Mr. Snell held the office of P. G. W. T. F.—“ain’t you going to tell us? What is this job? That is to say, what—er—may it be?”
source_text: “Well, Brother Snell,” said Mr. Noyes,--they were members together of a secret organization, of which Mr. Snell was P. G. W. T. F.,--“ain't you going to tell us? What--is this job? That is to say, what--er--is it?”

text: “Not here,” said Nancy hastily; “I dare not speak with you here. Come away—off the public road—down those steps yonder!”
source_text: “Not here,” said Nancy hurriedly, “I am afraid to speak to you here. Come away—out of the public road—down the steps yonder!”

text: “Do you know, Sydney,” said Mr. Stryver, fixing him with keen eyes and slowly passing a hand across his florid face: “do you know, I rather fancied, at the time, that you felt for the golden-haired doll, and were quick to understand what had become of the golden-haired doll?”
source_text: “Do you know, Sydney,” said Mr. Stryver, looking at him with sharp eyes, and slowly drawing a hand across his florid face: “do you know, I rather thought, at the time, that you sympathised with the golden-haired doll, and were quick to see what happened to the golden-haired doll?”

text: Yes, it had come to “Death to the Republic!” everywhere—no voice of dissent was heard. All England was marching against us! Truly, this was more than I had bargained for.
source_text: Yes, it was now “Death to the Republic!” everywhere--not a dissenting voice. All England was marching against us! Truly, this was more than I had bargained for.

text: “I am not here by chance,” he added, after a long silence; “nor have I learned all this tonight, for I knew it yesterday—yesterday only. Do you think that I have come to remind you of a promise?”
source_text: “I am not here by accident,” he added after a lengthened silence; “nor have I heard all this tonight, for I knew it yesterday—only yesterday. Do you guess that I have come to remind you of a promise?”

text: “The whole spree’s in her honour, isn’t it—eh?” said the old man. “Though he’d have it that it’s only to keep up o’ Christmas?”
source_text: “The spree is all in her honour, isn’t it—hey?” said the old man. “Although he made believe ’tis only keeping up o’ Christmas?”

text: “Oh, lady, lady!” she said, folding her hands with fervor before her face; “if there were more like you, there would be fewer like me,—there would, there would!”
source_text: “Oh, lady, lady!” she said, clasping her hands passionately before her face, “if there was more like you, there would be fewer like me,—there would—there would!”

text: “I went to Bath that night fully resolved to break my engagement with Mr. Troy. It was by reason of circumstances that arose after my arrival there that—that we were married. Now, do you not regard the matter in a different light?”
source_text: “I went to Bath that night in the full intention of breaking off my engagement to Mr. Troy. It was owing to circumstances which occurred after I got there that—that we were married. Now, do you see the matter in a new light?”

text: “I should have liked—I wish—you had never revealed yourself to me by forcing your way in here!” She tugged once more, and the gathers of her dress began to yield with a little crackle, like a miniature volley of musketry.
source_text: “I should have liked—I wish—you had never shown yourself to me by intruding here!” She pulled again, and the gathers of her dress began to give way like liliputian musketry.

text: “I cannot endure the thought of any son of mine making a bad marriage! Would that I had never lived to witness this; it is too cruel for me—it is beyond all that I ever imagined!” She turned toward the window. Her breathing came fast, and her lips were white, parted, and quivering.
source_text: “I hate the thought of any son of mine marrying badly! I wish I had never lived to see this; it is too much for me—it is more than I dreamt!” She turned to the window. Her breath was coming quickly, and her lips were pale, parted, and trembling.

text: “How often does he write to you? Where does he leave his letters—when does he see you? Ah, those letters of his! Will you tell me his name?”
source_text: “How often does he write to you? Where does he put his letters—when does he meet you? Ah, his letters! Do you tell me his name?”

text: “Whist!” says the tall man; “would ye be misled by physiognomy? My nose will do what it may within due bounds. Let us have these glasses filled again, for ’tis well to keep idiosyncrasies properly moistened, they being apt to decay in a dry moral atmosphere.”
source_text: “Whist!” says the long man; “would ye be led astray by physiognomy? Me nose will do what it can within bounds. Let us have these glasses filled again, for ’tis good to keep idiosyncrasies well moistened, they being subject to deterioration in a dry moral atmosphere.”

text: “Never,” returned Monks; “nor against me, neither. Look you here! But stir not a step nearer, or your life isn’t worth a bulrush.”
source_text: “Never,” rejoined Monks; “nor against me either. See here! But don’t move a step forward, or your life is not worth a bulrush.”

text: “Hush, hush! O, pray, Clym, do not, do not say it!” Thomasin implored, her terror breaking into sobs and tears; while Eustacia, on the farther side of the room, though her pale countenance kept its composure, writhed in her chair. Clym continued on, paying no heed to his cousin.
source_text: “Hush, hush! O, pray, Clym, don’t, don’t say it!” implored Thomasin, affrighted into sobs and tears; while Eustacia, at the other side of the room, though her pale face remained calm, writhed in her chair. Clym went on without heeding his cousin.

text: “My man, don’t be frightened,” said the dealer in red, his manner all at once growing mild. “I had forgotten you were here. It is only a strange way in which reddlemen go out of their minds for a moment; but they do no one any harm. And what did the lady say then?”
source_text: “My man, don’t you be afraid,” said the dealer in red, suddenly becoming gentle. “I forgot you were here. That’s only a curious way reddlemen have of going mad for a moment; but they don’t hurt anybody. And what did the lady say then?”

text: “Dear, dear—how the faces of nations change, and what strange things we live to witness nowadays! Yes—and it is much the same here. They have been speaking only just now of the missus’s strange goings-on.”
source_text: “Dear, dear—how the face of nations alter, and what we live to see nowadays! Yes—and ’tis the same here. They’ve been talking but now of the mis’ess’s strange doings.”

text: “Now you must set your best leg foremost, old lady!” whispered Sowerberry in the old woman’s ear; “we are somewhat late, and it will not answer to keep the clergyman waiting. Move on, my men—as briskly as you please!”
source_text: “Now, you must put your best leg foremost, old lady!” whispered Sowerberry in the old woman’s ear; “we are rather late; and it won’t do, to keep the clergyman waiting. Move on, my men,—as quick as you like!”

text: “There!” he said, setting the bundle down on the table. “See to that, and do as much as you can with it. It was trouble enough to get; I had thought I ought to have been here three hours ago.”
source_text: “There!” he said, laying the bundle on the table. “Take care of that, and do the most you can with it. It’s been trouble enough to get; I thought I should have been here, three hours ago.”

text: “Do you want to see how it happened?” he asked in a cheerful tone. I read the article. Its headings began where I had heard the buzzing stop the night before. It ended with these lines:
source_text: “Want to see how it happened?” he asked cheerily. I read the article. Its headlines began where I heard the buzzing leave off the night before. It closed with these lines:

text: “I was remarking to madame, with whom I had the pleasure of conversing as you came in, that, so I am told, there is—nor is it any marvel!—much sympathy and indignation in Saint Antoine touching the miserable fate of poor Gaspard.”
source_text: “I was saying to madame, with whom I had the pleasure of chatting when you entered, that they tell me there is--and no wonder!--much sympathy and anger in Saint Antoine, touching the unhappy fate of poor Gaspard.”

text: Moratorium," reflected Mr. Britling; "moratorium. What nonsense you talk! It means something that causes delay, of course. Nothing to do with death. Only a temporary suspension of payments.... Of course there must be a tremendous alteration in values....
source_text: "Moratorium," reflected Mr. Britling; "Moratorium. What nonsense you talk! It's something that delays, of course. Nothing to do with death. Just a temporary stoppage of payments.... Of course there's bound to be a tremendous change in values...."

text: “He used to go over there of a Sunday afternoon to pay a visit to his old acquaintance Andrew Brown, who played first clarinet there; a good enough man, but rather screechy in his music, if you can call it to mind?”
source_text: “He used to walk over there of a Sunday afternoon to visit his old acquaintance Andrew Brown, the first clarinet there; a good man enough, but rather screechy in his music, if you can mind?”

text: “Here he is!” Giles shouted, in a transport of excitement, up the stairway. “Here’s one of the thieves, ma’am! Here’s a thief, miss! Wounded, miss! I shot him, miss; and Brittles held the light.”
source_text: “Here he is!” bawled Giles, calling in a state of great excitement, up the staircase; “here’s one of the thieves, ma’am! Here’s a thief, miss! Wounded, miss! I shot him, miss; and Brittles held the light.”

text: “Indeed, indeed!” cried the Jew, who had come in unperceived by Oliver. “The whole matter is contained in a nutshell, my dear—in a nutshell, if you would have the Dodger’s word for it. Ha! ha! ha! He knows the catechism of his calling.”
source_text: “To be sure, to be sure!” said the Jew, who had entered unseen by Oliver. “It all lies in a nutshell my dear; in a nutshell, take the Dodger’s word for it. Ha! ha! ha! He understands the catechism of his trade.”

text: “My life is a burden apart from you,” he cried in a low voice. “I want you—I want you to let me tell you, again and again, that I love you!”
source_text: “My life is a burden without you,” he exclaimed, in a low voice. “I want you—I want you to let me say I love you again and again!”

text: “How kind and generous you are! Everyone else believes in her guilt, and that made me miserable, for I knew it was impossible; and to see everyone else so fatally prejudiced left me hopeless and despairing.” She wept.
source_text: “How kind and generous you are! every one else believes in her guilt, and that made me wretched, for I knew that it was impossible: and to see every one else prejudiced in so deadly a manner rendered me hopeless and despairing.” She wept.

text: Defarge came in, breathless, snatched off the red cap he wore, and glanced about him. “Listen, everywhere!” said madame once more. “Listen to him!” Defarge stood there, panting, with eager eyes and open mouths gathered behind him outside the door; and all who were within the wine-shop had risen to their feet.
source_text: Defarge came in breathless, pulled off a red cap he wore, and looked around him! “Listen, everywhere!” said madame again. “Listen to him!” Defarge stood, panting, against a background of eager eyes and open mouths, formed outside the door; all those within the wine-shop had sprung to their feet.

text: “I name none, I defy all! Here I stand, and I dare the chivalry of England to come forth against me—not as individuals, but in the mass!”
source_text: “I name none, I challenge all! Here I stand, and dare the chivalry of England to come against me--not by individuals, but in mass!”

text: “Begone! I will not listen to you. There can be no fellowship between you and me; we are enemies. Begone, or let us test our strength in combat, in which one of us must fall.”
source_text: “Begone! I will not hear you. There can be no community between you and me; we are enemies. Begone, or let us try our strength in a fight, in which one must fall.”

text: Why, in a single instant we were back in this world! Then, at the same moment, we read the same startled thought in each other’s eyes: more than two weeks had passed, and that ship had not yet returned!
source_text: Why, we were back in this world in one instant! Then we looked the same startled thought into each other's eyes at the same moment; more than two weeks gone, and that ship not back yet!

text: Those among the farmers with whom she had no intercourse, and they were by far the greater number, were ever asking one another, “Who is she?” The answer would be—
source_text: Those of the farmers with whom she had no dealings (by far the greater part) were continually asking each other, “Who is she?” The reply would be—

text: “A murrain on the young devils!” cried the stranger; “I mean one of them: a meek-looking, pale-faced boy, who was put apprentice down here to a coffin-maker—I wish he had made his coffin, and screwed him in it—and who afterward ran away to London, as was supposed.”
source_text: “A murrain on the young devils!” cried the stranger; “I speak of one; a meek-looking, pale-faced boy, who was apprenticed down here, to a coffin-maker—I wish he had made his coffin, and screwed his body in it—and who afterwards ran away to London, as it was supposed.”

text: “I thought as much! Maryann and Liddy and Temperance—now I forbid you to imagine such things. You know I care not the least for Mr. Troy—not I. Everybody knows how greatly I despise him.—Yes,” repeated the wayward young lady, “_despise_ him!”
source_text: “I thought so! Maryann and Liddy and Temperance—now I forbid you to suppose such things. You know I don’t care the least for Mr. Troy—not I. Everybody knows how much I hate him.—Yes,” repeated the froward young person, “_hate_ him!”

text: “God be thanked for that single mercy, yes—these three hours. How shall I ever render in full the debt of my gratitude? My heart is so full of it that it seems ready to burst for the sleep he is sleeping now.”
source_text: “God be thanked for that one charity, yes--these three hours. Where shall I pay to the full, my gratitude! for my heart is bursting with it for that sleep he sleepeth now.”

text: “Oh yes,” she said hastily. “I know all that. But do not speak of it—seven years, or even six—where may we all be by that time?”
source_text: “Oh yes,” she said, quickly. “I know all that. But don’t talk of it—seven or six years—where may we all be by that time?”

text: “Eh?” exclaimed the brazen Stryver. “Did he, indeed? Is that the kind of man he is? Let us have a look at his infamous name. D--n the fellow!”
source_text: “Hey?” cried the blatant Stryver. “Did he though? Is that the sort of fellow? Let us look at his infamous name. D--n the fellow!”

text: “Your letter was in the _Nation_ the other day,” she said. “Why must you let yourself be drawn into arguments? I wanted to hurry into the _Nation_ and gather you up and brush the anger off you, and carry you away from it all—into some still, beautiful place.”
source_text: "There was your letter in the _Nation_ the other day," she said. "Why _do_ you get drawn into arguments? I wanted to rush into the _Nation_ and pick you up and wipe the anger off you, and carry you out of it all--into some quiet beautiful place."

text: “And the place—the mad den, wherever it lay—in which wretched drabs brought forth the life and health so often denied to themselves—bore puny children for the parish to rear; and cloaked their shame, rot ’em in the grave!”
source_text: “And the place, the crazy hole, wherever it was, in which miserable drabs brought forth the life and health so often denied to themselves—gave birth to puling children for the parish to rear; and hid their shame, rot ’em in the grave!”

text: The shell flung me down and didn’t hurt me in the least. I was dazed for a moment, and earth was showered over me, and when I rose onto my knees I saw Jewell lying about six yards away—and his legs were all smashed to pieces. Ugh! Pulped!
source_text: "The shell knocked me over, and didn't hurt me a bit. I was a little stunned, and some dirt was thrown over me, and when I got up on my knees I saw Jewell lying about six yards off--and his legs were all smashed about. Ugh! Pulped!

text: “Don’t you _understand_? Land of—why, you see—you see—why, good gracious, can’t you make out a little thing like that? Can’t you see the difference between your—_why_ do you look so innocent and idiotic!”
source_text: “Don't _understand_? Land of--why, you see--you see--why, great Scott, can't you understand a little thing like that? Can't you understand the difference between your--_why_ do you look so innocent and idiotic!”

text: “You find it no easy matter to counsel me?” said Mr. Lorry. “I perfectly understand it to be a delicate question. And yet I think—” Thereupon he shook his head, and came to a pause.
source_text: “You do not find it easy to advise me?” said Mr. Lorry. “I quite understand it to be a nice question. And yet I think--” And there he shook his head, and stopped.

text: “Yes, yes, I suppose that is about the extent of it. Do not be troubled by my seeming excited; I am not. Let us turn the subject. Now, as for this castle, with forty-five princesses in it and three ogres at its head, tell me—where is this harem?”
source_text: “Yes, yes, I reckon that's about the size of it. Don't mind my seeming excited; I'm not. Let us change the subject. Now as to this castle, with forty-five princesses in it, and three ogres at the head of it, tell me--where is this harem?”

text:
Here come I, a Turkish knight,  
Who learnt in Turkey’s land to fight;  
I’ll meet this man with heart so bold:  
If his blood runs hot, I’ll make it cold!
source_text: “Here come I, a Turkish Knight, Who learnt in Turkish land to fight; I’ll fight this man with courage bold: If his blood’s hot I’ll make it cold!”

text: “No marriage for me yet for many a year; if ever it comes to pass, it shall be for reasons very, very different from those you imagine, or that others will believe! Now fetch me my cloak, for it is time we were gone.”
source_text: “No marrying for me yet for many a year; if ever, ’twill be for reasons very, very different from those you think, or others will believe! Now get my cloak, for it is time to go.”

text: “Never! Do you?” said Bathsheba, laughing a little, though not without a certain serious alarm at this Amazonian image of herself. “I hope I am not a bold sort of maid—mannish?” she added, with some uneasiness.
source_text: “Never! do you?” said Bathsheba, slightly laughing, though somewhat seriously alarmed by this Amazonian picture of herself. “I hope I am not a bold sort of maid—mannish?” she continued with some anxiety.

text: “I’m here, miss,” said Mr. Giles. “Don’t be frightened, miss; I’m not much hurt. He didn’t put up any very desperate resistance, miss! I was soon too much for him.”
source_text: “I’m here, miss,” replied Mr. Giles. “Don’t be frightened, miss; I ain’t much injured. He didn’t make a very desperate resistance, miss! I was soon too many for him.”

text: “I see little of ‘work’ in that,” he said. “A minister is expected to preach a hundred and four sermons in every year, and there are plenty more whence they come. What is one sermon more or less, when the stock costs nothing? It is like carting gravel from the pit.”
source_text: “I don't see much 'job' in that,” he said; “a minister's supposed to preach a hundred and four sermons in each and every year, and there's plenty more where they come from. What's one sermon more or less, when stock costs nothing? It's like wheeling gravel from the pit.”

text: A particularly valuable application of ITL is in conversational virtual assistants (e.g., Apple Siri, Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa). These systems have been widely adopted by end users to carry out tasks across a range of domains through natural language dialogue. However, a major limitation of such systems is that their task completion and language understanding abilities are restricted to a small set of preprogrammed tasks (Li et al., 2018b). This limited coverage is insufficient for the diverse “long-tail” of user needs and preferences (Li et al., 2017a). Although some software agents expose APIs that allow third-party developers to create new “skills” for them, doing so requires substantial programming expertise and the appropriate APIs, and is therefore inaccessible to the vast majority of end users.
source_text: A particularly useful application of ITL is for conversational virtual assistants (e.g., Apple Siri, Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa). These systems have been widely adopted by end users to perform tasks in a variety of domains through natural language conversation. However, a key limitation of these systems is that their task fulfillment and language understanding capabilities are limited to a small set of pre-programmed tasks (Li et al., 2018b; . This limited support is not adequate for the diverse "long-tail" of user needs and preferences (Li et al., 2017a) . Although some software agents provide APIs to enable thirdparty developers to develop new "skills" for them, this requires significant programming expertise and relevant APIs, and therefore is not usable by the vast majority of end users.

text: We begin by extracting coarse-grained events and arguments with a Bi-LSTM CRF model and a CNN-based model (Zhang et al., 2018b) for three languages, and then identify fine-grained event types by applying verb-based rules, context-based rules, and argument-based rules (Li et al., 2019b). We also extract FrameNet frames (Chen et al., 2010) from English corpora to further enrich the fine-grained events.
source_text: We start by extracting coarse-grained events and arguments using a Bi-LSTM CRF model and a CNNbased model (Zhang et al., 2018b) for three languages, and then detect the fine-grained event types by applying verb-based rules, context-based rules, and argument-based rules (Li et al., 2019b) . We also extract FrameNet frames (Chen et al., 2010) in English corpora to enrich the fine-grained events.

text: At each iteration of the algorithm, it first randomly samples a pair \((x, z)\) from the training template database \(T\) (line 3). It then examines every pair \((x,\hat{z})\) in \(T\) to determine whether it is slot-inconsistent with respect to \((x, z)\), and adds any pair within a specified distance (a hyperparameter) to the set \(Z\) (lines 5–11). This distance is usually set to a small value so that the selected samples remain sufficiently close to \((x, z)\); in practice, we set it to 2. Finally, it randomly samples from \(Z\) (line 12) and inserts the result into the output set \(\Omega\). This bootstrapping procedure terminates when the number of generated samples reaches a fixed limit \(K\).
source_text: At each turn of the algorithm, it first randomly samples (line 3-th) a pair (x, z), from training template data base of T . Then for every pair (x,ẑ) in T , it measures if the pair (x,ẑ) is slot-inconsistent with respect to (x, z), and adds the pair that is within a certain distance (a hyper parameter) to a set Z (line 5-th to 11-th). is usually set to a small number so that the selected samples are close enough to (x, z). In practice, we set it to 2. Finally, it does a random sampling (line 12-th) on Z and insert its return into the output set Ω. Such bootstrapping process stops when the number of generated samples reaches a certain limit K.

text: We believe the time is now to develop a unified toolkit that supports research in both E2E and cascaded approaches. We introduce ESPnet-ST, a toolkit that implements many recent models for E2E-ST, as well as the ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST. Our aim is to provide a toolkit in which researchers can easily incorporate and evaluate new ideas across different approaches. Recent studies suggest that pre-training, multi-task learning, and transfer learning are important techniques for obtaining improved results in E2E-ST (Bérard et al., 2018; Anastasopoulos and Chiang, 2018; Bansal et al., 2019; . Therefore, a unified toolkit that allows researchers to seamlessly mix and match different ASR and MT models when training both E2E-ST and Cascade-ST systems would facilitate research in this area. 1 ESPnet-ST is especially designed for the ST task. ESPnet was originally developed for the 1 There are many excellent toolkits that support both ASR and MT tasks (see Table 1 ). However, it is not always straightforward to use them for E2E-ST and Cascade-ST, because of incompatible training/inference pipelines across modules or the absence of detailed preprocessing/training scripts.
source_text: We believe the time is ripe to develop a unified toolkit that facilitates research in both E2E and cascaded approaches. We present ESPnet-ST, a toolkit that implements many of the recent models for E2E-ST, as well as the ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST. Our goal is to provide a toolkit where researchers can easily incorporate and test new ideas under different approaches. Recent research suggests that pre-training, multi-task learning, and transfer learning are important techniques for achieving improved results for E2E-ST (Bérard et al., 2018; Anastasopoulos and Chiang, 2018; Bansal et al., 2019; . Thus, a unified toolkit that enables researchers to seamlessly mix-and-match different ASR and MT models in training both E2E-ST and Cascade-ST systems would facilitate research in the field. 1 ESPnet-ST is especially designed to target the ST task. ESPnet was originally developed for the 1 There exist many excellent toolkits that support both ASR and MT tasks (see Table 1 ). However, it is not always straightforward to use them for E2E-ST and Cascade-ST, due to incompatible training/inference pipelines in different modules or lack of detailed preprocessing/training scripts.

text: The log-partition employs ⊕, ⊗ = (LSE, +), but alternative choices can be used in its place. For example, using the log-max semiring (max, +) in the forward algorithm produces the maximum score. As noted earlier, its gradient with respect to is the argmax sequence, eliminating the need for a separate argmax (Viterbi) algorithm. Some distributional quantities cannot be obtained directly through gradient identities, yet they still rely on a forward-backward-style computation pattern. For instance, sampling first requires calculating the log-partition term and then sampling each component, as in forward filtering/backward sampling. This value can be computed by replacing each backpropagation operation for the to instead produce a sample. Table 2 lists the semirings and backpropagation procedures used to compute different quantities of interest. We observe that many of the terms needed in the case study can be computed with variant semirings, removing the need for specialized algorithms.
source_text: The log-partition utilizes ⊕, ⊗ = (LSE, +), but we can substitute alternatives. For instance, utilizing the log-max semiring (max, +) in the forward algorithm yields the max score. As we have seen, its gradient with respect to is the argmax sequence, negating the need for a separate argmax (Viterbi) algorithm. Some distributional properties cannot be computed directly through gradient identities but still use a forward-backward style compute structure. For instance, sampling requires first computing the log-partition term and then sampling each part, (forward filtering / backward sampling). We can compute this value by overriding each backpropagation operation for the to instead compute a sample. Table 2 shows the set of semirings and backpropagation steps for computing different terms of interest. We note that many of the terms necessary in the case-study can be computed with variant semirings, negating the need for specialized algorithms.

text: Noisy User Input in Semantic Parsing. Although it is absent from most large-scale text-to-SQL benchmarks, noisy user input has often been encountered and addressed by the semantic parsing community. Underspecification (Archangeli, 1988) and vagueness (Varzi, 2001) have a strong basis in linguistic theory. Lexicon-based semantic parsers (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Roberts and Patra, 2017) may reject an input if lexicon matching fails. Other approaches for handling untranslatable user input include inference and default generation (Setlur et al., 2019), paraphrasing (Arthur et al., 2015; Arthur et al., 2016), verification (Arthur et al., 2015), and confidence estimation. We adopt a data augmentation and discriminative learning-based approach, which has shown superior performance in related domains (Rajpurkar et al., 2018).
source_text: Noisy User Input in Semantic Parsing. Despite being absent from most large-scale text-to-SQL benchmarks, noisy user input has been frequently encountered and battled with by the semantic parsing community. Underspecification (Archangeli, 1988) and vagueness (Varzi, 2001 ) have solid linguistic theory foundation. Lexicon-based semantic parsers (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Roberts and Patra, 2017) may reject the input if the lexicon match is unsuccessful. Other approaches for handling untranslatable user input include inference and generating defaults (Setlur et al., 2019) , paraphrasing (Arthur et al., 2015 (Arthur et al., , 2016 , verification (Arthur et al., 2015) and confidence estimation . We adopt a dataaugmentation and discriminative learning based approach, which has demonstrated superior performance in related domains (Rajpurkar et al., 2018)

text: Moreover, the attention mechanism was introduced in MLC by Mullenbach et al. (2018) for ICD coding. Rios and Kavuluru (2018) train label representations with a basic GraphCNN and perform multi-label attention with residual shortcuts. AttentionXML (You et al., 2019) transforms MLC into a multi-label attention LCL model using label clusters. Huang et al. (2019) enhances HMCN (Wehrmann et al., 2018) with label attention at each level. By contrast, our HiAGM-LA applies multi-label attention within a single model and uses a simplified structure encoder, thereby reducing computational complexity.
source_text: Moreover, the attention mechanism is introduced in MLC by Mullenbach et al. (2018) for ICD coding. Rios and Kavuluru (2018) trains label representation through basic GraphCNN and conducts mutli-label attention with residual shortcuts. At-tentionXML (You et al., 2019) converts MLC to a multi-label attention LCL model by label clusters. Huang et al. (2019) improves HMCN (Wehrmann et al., 2018) with label attention per level. Our HiAGM-LA, however, employs multi-label attention in a single model with a simplified structure encoder, reducing the computational complexity.

text: To handle ambiguous and untranslatable input questions, PHOTON uses a discriminatively trained classifier to identify user inputs for which a SQL mapping cannot be determined immediately. This includes questions that are incomplete (e.g. What is the total?), ambiguous or vague (e.g. Show me homes with good schools), outside the representational scope of SQL (e.g. How many tourists visited all of the 10 attractions?), or simply noisy (e.g. Cyrus teaches physics in department).
source_text: In order to handle ambiguous and untranslatable input questions, PHOTON adopts a discriminatively trained classifier to detect user input to which a SQL mapping cannot be immediately determined. This covers questions that are incomplete (e.g. What is the total?), ambiguous or vague (e.g. Show me homes with good schools), beyond the representation scope of SQL (e.g. How many tourists visited all of the 10 attractions?), or simply noisy (e.g. Cyrus teaches physics in department).

text: Another way in which SUGILITE exploits GUI groundings in natural language instructions is by inferring task parameters and their possible values. This enables the agent to learn generalized procedures—for example, how to order any type of beverage from Starbucks—from a demonstration of a specific task instance, such as ordering an iced cappuccino. SUGILITE accomplishes this by matching the user utterance (e.g., “order a cup of iced cappuccino”) with the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text “Iced Cappuccino”) and with the arguments of the demonstrated actions (e.g., put “Iced Cappuccino” into a search box) to find correspondences. Through this process, different parts of the utterance are grounded to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then examines the hierarchical GUI structure at the time of demonstration and searches for alternative GUI elements that are structurally parallel to the original target GUI element. In this manner, it derives other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks shown in the same menu as “Iced Cappuccino”.
source_text: Another way SUGILITE leverages GUI groundings in the natural language instructions is to infer task parameters and their possible values. This allows the agent to learn generalized procedures (e.g., to order any kind of beverage from Starbucks) from a demonstration of a specific instance of the task (e.g., ordering an iced cappuccino). SUGILITE achieves this by comparing the user utterance (e.g., "order a cup of iced cappuccino") against the data descriptions of the target UI elements (e.g., click on the menu item that has the text "Iced Cappuccino") and the arguments (e.g., put "Iced Cappuccino" into a search box) of the demonstrated actions for matches. This process grounds different parts in the utterances to specific actions in the demonstrated procedure. It then analyzes the hierarchical structure of GUI at the time of demonstration, and looks for alternative GUI elements that are in parallel to the original target GUI element structurally. In this way, it extracts the other possible values for the identified parameter, such as the names of all the other drinks displayed in the same menu as "Iced Cappuccino"

text: Psychologists, on the one hand, value such lexicons as a controlled set of stimuli for designing experiments, for example, to investigate patterns of lexical access or the structure of memory (Monnier and Syssau, 2008). NLP researchers, on the other hand, use them to enrich the emotional content of word embeddings (Yu et al., 2017; Khosla et al., 2018), as additional input for sentence-level emotion models so that even the most sophisticated neural network achieves better performance (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017; De Bruyne et al., 2019), or rely on them in a keyword-spotting approach when no training data is available, for instance in studies dealing with historical language stages.
source_text: Psychologists, on the one hand, value such lexicons as a controlled set of stimuli for designing experiments, e.g., to investigate patterns of lexical access or the structure of memory Monnier and Syssau, 2008) . NLP researchers, on the other hand, use them to augment the emotional loading of word embeddings (Yu et al., 2017; Khosla et al., 2018) , as additional input to sentence-level emotion models so that the performance of even the most sophisticated neural network gets boosted (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017; De Bruyne et al., 2019) , or rely on them in a keyword-spotting approach when no training data is available, e.g., for studies dealing with historical language stages .

text: Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008), are concerned with parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007), CRF++ (Kudo, 2005), and NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018), provide inference for a fixed collection of widely used models, typically linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or TurboParser (Martins et al., 2010), rely on external solvers for inference, chiefly MAP inference, including integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for instance languages integrated with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019), enable specification and inference over some discrete domains. More ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) support declarative descriptions of dynamic programming algorithms to enable inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct adopts a different strategy, integrating a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning framework. We start by motivating this design with a case study.
source_text: Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008) , focus on parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007) , CRF++ (Kudo, 2005) , or NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018) , implement inference for a fixed set of popular models, usually linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or Tur-boParser (Martins et al., 2010), utilize external solvers for (primarily MAP) inference such as integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for example languages that integrate with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019) , allow for specification and inference over some discrete domains. Most ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) allow for declarative specifications of dynamic programming algorithms to support inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct takes a different approach and integrates a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning system. We begin by motivating this approach with a case study.

text: Even if the data itself is obtained legally and ethically, the technology, when used for unintended purposes, can lead to harmful outcomes. This demo organizes multimedia information to help users navigate and understand international events described across multiple sources. We have also applied the underlying technology to data that could support natural disaster relief efforts (Zhang et al., 2018a) 19, and we are currently exploring the application of the models, with different data, to scientific literature and drug discovery (Wang et al., 2020) 20.
source_text: Even if the data itself is obtained legally and ethically, the technology when use for unintended purposes can result in harmful outcomes. This demo organizes multimedia information to aid in navigating and understanding international events described in multiple sources. We have also applied the underlying technology on data that would aid natural disaster relief efforts (Zhang et al., 2018a) 19 and we are currently exploring the application of the models (with different data) to scientific literature and drug discovery (Wang et al., 2020) 20 .

text: Several earlier studies have explored the extraction of safety-related information on supplements and supplement interactions from different kinds of text. Zhang et al. (2015) use machine learning methods to filter supplement interaction relations in SemMedDB, a database of relations extracted from Medline articles. Jiang et al. (2017) build a model to detect adverse effects associated with dietary supplements as reported by consumers on Twitter, and identify 191 adverse effects involving 4 dietary supplements. Fan et al. (2016) and Fan and Zhang (2018) examine unstructured clinical notes to predict whether a patient has started, continued, or discontinued a dietary supplement, which may serve as a useful component for identifying adverse effects in clinical notes, as the same authors later attempted in Fan et al. (2017) for the drug warfarin. Another study proposes using topic models to analyze adverse effects of dietary supplements mentioned in the Dietary Supplement Label Database, and shows that Latent Dirichlet Allocation models (Blei et al., 2003) can group dietary supplements with similar adverse effects based on their labels. To the best of our knowledge, no other studies have examined the task of sentence-level identification of SDI/SSI evidence from the scientific literature. Likewise, no prior work has investigated whether labeled DDI data can be used for transfer learning to SDI/SSI identification.
source_text: Several prior studies have experimented with extracting safety information of supplements and supplement interactions from various forms of text. Zhang et al. (2015) employ machine learning techniques to filter supplement interaction relationships in SemMedDB, a database of relationships extracted from Medline articles. Jiang et al. (2017) develop a model for identifying adverse effects related to dietary supplements as reported by consumers on Twitter, and discover 191 adverse effects pertaining to 4 dietary supplements. Fan et al. (2016) and Fan and Zhang (2018) analyze unstructured clinical notes to predict whether a patient started, continued or discontinued a dietary supplement, which can be useful as a building block for identifying adverse effects in clinical notes (as attempted by the same authors in Fan et al. (2017) for the drug warfarin). proposes using topic models to analyze the adverse effects of dietary supplements as mentioned in the Dietary Supplement Label Database, and finds that Latent Dirichlet Allocation models (Blei et al., 2003) can be used to group dietary supplements with similar adverse effects based on their labels. As far as we know, there are no other studies investigating the task of sentence-level identification of SDI/SSI evidence from the scientific literature. No previous work has investigated the utility of using labeled DDI data for transfer learning to SDI/SSI identification.

text: Model architecture: As the name suggests, RoBERTa-DDI relies on pre-trained RoBERTa representations (Liu et al., 2019) to encode the input sequences. For further details on BERT and the transformer architecture, we refer readers to Liu et al. 2019, Devlin et al. (2018), and Vaswani et al. (2017). In RoBERTa-DDI, we attach a dropout layer followed by a single feedforward output layer with a softmax nonlinearity. This layer takes as input the [CLS] token representation from the top transformer layer and produces probabilities over the labels {0, 1}, where 1 denotes an interaction.
source_text: Model architecture: As the name implies, RoBERTa-DDI uses the pre-trained RoBERTa representations (Liu et al., 2019) to encode input sequences. We refer readers to Liu et al. 2019, Devlin et al. (2018) , and Vaswani et al. (2017) for more details on BERT and transformer architecture. For the RoBERTa-DDI model, we add a dropout layer followed by one feedforward (output) layer with a softmax non-linearity, which takes the representation of the [CLS] token at the top transformer layer as input and outputs probabilities for labels {0, 1}, where 1 indicates an interaction.

text: A relatively recent approach to generating novel labels is emotion representation mapping (ERM), an annotation projection method that translates ratings from one emotion format into another, for example by mapping VAD labels to BE5, or the reverse (Hoffmann et al., 2012; Hahn, 2016, 2018a; Alarcão and Fonseca, 2017; Landowska, 2018; Zhou et al., 2020; Park et al., 2019). In our work, ERM is used to add further emotion variables to the source lexicon, but ERM by itself can neither expand a lexicon’s coverage nor adapt it to a different language.
source_text: A relatively new way of generating novel labels is emotion representation mapping (ERM), an annotation projection that translates ratings from one emotion format into another, e.g., mapping VAD labels into BE5, or vice versa (Hoffmann et al., 2012; Hahn, 2016, 2018a; Alarcão and Fonseca, 2017; Landowska, 2018; Zhou et al., 2020; Park et al., 2019) . While our work uses ERM to add additional emotion variables to the source lexicon, ERM alone can neither increase the coverage of a lexicon, nor adapt it to another language.

text: In NLP, the absence of reliable methods for estimating the MWF of derivatives creates a bottleneck for generative models, especially in languages with rich derivational morphology; for example, although inflected forms can be translated by producing morphologically corresponding forms in the target language (Minkov et al., 2007), the generation of derivatives remains a major challenge for machine translation systems (Sreelekha and Bhattacharyya, 2018). Comparable difficulties also arise in automatic language generation (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018).
source_text: In NLP, the lack of reliable ways to estimate the MWF of derivatives poses a bottleneck for generative models, particularly in languages exhibiting a rich derivational morphology; e.g., while inflected forms can be translated by generating morphologically corresponding forms in the target language (Minkov et al., 2007) , generating derivatives is still a major challenge for machine translation systems (Sreelekha and Bhattacharyya, 2018) . Similar problems exist in the area of automatic language generation (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018) .

text: Dev Test BERT LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) scores 49.3 and 44.2, RoBERTa LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) scores 53.7 and 49.7, and RoBERTa-LARGE + AdvTrain reaches 57.1 on both. GLUE includes sis (Socher et al., 2013), text similarity (Cer et al., 2017), paraphrase detection (Dolan and Brockett, 2005), and natural language inference (NLI) Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampiccolo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009; Levesque et al., 2012; Williams et al., 2018). The range of tasks makes GLUE particularly well suited for assessing the generalization and robustness of NLU models.
source_text: Dev Test BERT LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) 49.3 44.2 RoBERTa LARGE (Nie et al., 2019) 53.7 49.7 RoBERTa-LARGE + AdvTrain 57.1 57.1 sis (Socher et al., 2013 ), text similarity (Cer et al., 2017) , paraphrase detection (Dolan and Brockett, 2005) , and natural language inference (NLI) Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampiccolo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009; Levesque et al., 2012; Williams et al., 2018) . The diversity of the tasks makes GLUE very suitable for evaluating the generalization and robustness of NLU models.

text: Marvin and Linzen (2018) introduce a dataset of minimal-pair sentences intended to assess the syntactic generalization abilities of language models. However, the dataset’s syntactic scope is restricted to a small number of phenomena: subject-verb agreement, reflexive anaphor licensing, and negative polarity items. Warstadt et al. (2020) present a much larger dataset that aggregates a wide range of targeted syntactic evaluations from earlier work, known as BLiMP. As with the Marvin and Linzen dataset, BLiMP comprises minimal-pair sentences that differ in grammaticality, following the standard illustrated in Examples (1) and (2). The BLiMP evaluation asks language models to assign a higher total probability to the grammatical sentence in (1) than to the ungrammatical one in (2). The authors construct abstract templates that define grammatical-ungrammatical pairs for numerous linguistic phenomena and then generate example sentences from these templates.
source_text: Marvin and Linzen (2018) release a dataset of minimal-pair sentences designed to test language models' syntactic generalization capabilities. However, the syntactic coverage of the dataset is limited to a small set of phenomena: subject-verb agreement, reflexive anaphor licensing, and negative polarity items. Warstadt et al. (2020) release a large dataset aggregating a broad collection of targeted syntactic evaluations from prior research, known as BLiMP. Like the Marvin and Linzen dataset, BLiMP consists of a collection of minimal-pair sentences which contrast in grammaticality, following the standard shown in Examples (1) and (2). The BLiMP evaluation requires that language models assign a higher total probability to the grammatical (1) than the ungrammatical (2). The authors design abstract templates which specify grammatical- ungrammatical pairs for many linguistic phenomena, and then generate example sentences based on these templates.

text: Our methodology also resembles prior work that models word emotion across historical language stages (Cook and Stevenson, 2010; Hamilton et al., 2016; Hellrich et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). Research in this area typically begins with a set of seed words presumed to have temporally stable affective meanings (in our case, stability across translation is assumed) and then applies distributional methods to infer emotion ratings in the target language stage. However, gold data for the target language stage is usually unavailable, which often makes evaluation against human judgment impossible. By contrast, we propose several alternative evaluation setups here as an integral part of our methodology.
source_text: Our methodology also resembles previous work which models word emotion for historical language stages (Cook and Stevenson, 2010; Hamilton et al., 2016; Hellrich et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019) . Work in this direction typically comes up with a set of seed words with assumingly temporally stable affective meaning (our work assumes stability against translation) and then uses distributional methods to derive emotion ratings in the target language stage. However, gold data for the target language (stage) is usually inaccessible, often preventing evaluation against human judgment. In contrast, we here propose several alternative evaluation set-ups as an integral part of our methodology.

text:
Some keep the Sabbath by going to church;
I keep it by remaining home,
With a bobolink as choirmaster,
And an orchard serving as a dome.
source_text:
Some keep the Sabbath going to church;
I keep it staying at home,
With a bobolink for a chorister,
And an orchard for a dome.

text:
And all this, though most serene,—
Eager, at a breath, to languish,—
Was a state no less a dream
Than the peace he could not deem,
Or the care he could not banish.
source_text:
And all this, though quite ideal,—
Ready at a breath to vanish,—
Was a state not more unreal
Than the peace he could not feel,
Or the care he could not banish.

text:
borne . . . dies. Observe how the line’s cadence answers
the meaning. It appears to lift and sink, and lift and sink once more.
source_text:
ll. 28-9. borne . . . dies. Notice how the cadence of the line fits
the sense. It seems to rise and fall and rise and fall again.

text:
We deem the bibles and the religions divine—I do not say they are not divine,
I say they have all sprung forth from you, and may yet spring forth from you still,
It is not they that bestow the life, it is you that bestow the life,
Leaves are not more shed from the trees, nor trees from the earth,
than they are shed forth out of you.
source_text:
We consider bibles and religions divine--I do not say they are not divine,
I say they have all grown out of you, and may grow out of you still,
It is not they who give the life, it is you who give the life,
Leaves are not more shed from the trees, or trees from the earth,
than they are shed out of you.

text:
The neighbors still have no suspicion!
The woods share a smile --
Orchard, and buttercup, and bird --
In only such a little while!
source_text:
The neighbors do not yet suspect!
The woods exchange a smile --
Orchard, and buttercup, and bird --
In such a little while!

text:
At night he oft would start and wake  
As doth a lover, and would begin  
In wild, unruly strain to make  
Songs of the moor, the glen, the rocky lake,  
And of the heart of man—
source_text:
At night he oft would start and wake
Like a lover, and began
In a wild measure songs to make
On moor, and glen, and rocky lake,
And on the heart of man—

text:
Ye turn to Heaven for help in your distress;  
Alas, that ye, the mighty and the wise,  
Who, if ye dared, might claim no less  
Than what ye deem of power, should dread the lies  
Which thou and thou didst forge as mysteries  
To blind your slaves:—consider now your own thought,  
An idle and a ruthless sacrifice  
Ye now prepare, for a vain idol wrought  
From the fears and hate that vain desires have brought.
source_text:
‘Ye turn to Heaven for aid in your distress;
Alas, that ye, the mighty and the wise,
Who, if ye dared, might not aspire to less
Than ye conceive of power, should fear the lies
Which thou, and thou, didst frame for mysteries
To blind your slaves:—consider your own thought,
An empty and a cruel sacrifice
Ye now prepare, for a vain idol wrought
Out of the fears and hate which vain desires have brought.

text:
(Mother! with subtle, stern regard, and the naked sword within your hand,  
I saw at last you spurn all dealing save direct with single souls.)
source_text:
(Mother! with subtle sense severe, with the naked sword in your hand,
I saw you at last refuse to treat but directly with individuals.)

text:
Cold lash of water at the ferry-wharf, slush and ice on the river,  
half-thawed mud in the streets,  
A dull, dispirited sky above, the brief dying light of December,  
A hearse and stagecoaches, the burial of an old Broadway stage-driver,  
the procession made up mostly of drivers.
source_text:
Cold dash of waves at the ferry-wharf, posh and ice in the river,
half-frozen mud in the streets,
A gray discouraged sky overhead, the short last daylight of December,
A hearse and stages, the funeral of an old Broadway stage-driver,
the cortege mostly drivers.

text:
O ye that mind the gospel fold,  
There’s Duncan dour, and Peebles bold;  
But chiefly thee, apostle Auld,  
We put our trust in thee,  
That thou wilt shape them, warm and cold,  
Till all agree.
source_text:
A’ ye wha tent the gospel fauld,
There’s Duncan deep, an’ Peebles shaul,
But chiefly thou, apostle Auld,
We trust in thee,
That thou wilt work them, het an’ cauld,
Till they agree.

text:
Secure from treason bred within,
No traitorous guile need it fear;
Itself its sovereign, of itself
The soul should stand in awe.
source_text:
Secure against its own,
No treason it can fear;
Itself its sovereign, of itself
The soul should stand in awe.

text:
While The Fall of Hyperion filled his evenings,  
his mornings bent to a satiric fairy-poem, The Cap and Bells,  
wrought in the measure of The Faerie Queene; yet that old music  
sat ill on its theme: satire was not his natural gift, and the poem has scant intrinsic grace.
source_text:
Whilst The Fall of Hyperion occupied his evenings his mornings were
spent over a satirical fairy-poem, The Cap and Bells, in the metre of
the Faerie Queene. This metre, however, was ill-suited to the subject;
satire was not natural to him, and the poem has little intrinsic merit.

text:
SIXTH SPIRIT:
Ah, sister! Desolation is a thing most fine and frail:
It walks not on the earth, it drifts not through the air,
But comes with soothing footfall, and fans with silent pale
Its tender hopes, that in their bosoms best and gentlest bear;
Who, lulled to false repose by the waving plumes above
And the music-moving cadence of its soft and restless feet,
Dream visions of ethereal bliss, and name the monster, Love,
And wake, and find but shadow-Pain, as he whom now we greet.
source_text:
SIXTH SPIRIT:
Ah, sister! Desolation is a delicate thing:
It walks not on the earth, it floats not on the air,
But treads with lulling footstep, and fans with silent wing
The tender hopes which in their hearts the best and gentlest bear;
Who, soothed to false repose by the fanning plumes above
And the music-stirring motion of its soft and busy feet,
Dream visions of aereal joy, and call the monster, Love,
And wake, and find the shadow Pain, as he whom now we greet.

text:
O rough, bold, quick-witted Rankine,  
The wale o’ chaps for fun an’ drinkin!  
There’s mony a pious soul is thinkin,  
Your dreams and sleights  
Will send you, Korah-wise, a-sinkin  
Clean to auld Nick’s.
source_text:
O Rough, rude, ready-witted Rankine,
The wale o’ cocks for fun an’ drinkin!
There’s mony godly folks are thinkin,
Your dreams and tricks
Will send you, Korah-like, a-sinkin
Straught to auld Nick’s.

text:
Who has fulfilled his day’s appointed task? who shall first be done with his evening meal?
Who would desire to stroll beside me?
source_text:
Who has done his day’s work? who will soonest be through with his supper?
Who wishes to walk with me?

text:
And so he was silent, and that very night,  
While Tom lay in slumber, he witnessed such sight!—  
That thousands of sweepers, Dick, Joe, Ned, and Jack,  
Were all of them coffined in darkness of black.
source_text:
And so he was quiet, and that very night,
As Tom was a-sleeping, he had such a sight!—
That thousands of sweepers, Dick, Joe, Ned, and Jack,
Were all of them locked up in coffins of black.

text:
What chemistry!
That the winds are not truly contagious,
That this is no deception, this clear green-wash of the sea, which
is so longing after me,
That it is safe to let it lick my bare body everywhere with its tongues,
That it will not threaten me with the fevers that have settled
within it,
That all remains clean, forever and ever,
That the cool draught from the well tastes so sweet,
That blackberries are so rich and juicy,
That the fruits of the apple-grove and the orange-grove, that
melons, grapes, peaches, plums, none of them will poison me,
That when I lie back on the grass I do not contract any illness,
Though perhaps every blade of grass springs from what was once
an illness that could be caught.
source_text:
What chemistry!
That the winds are really not infectious,
That this is no cheat, this transparent green-wash of the sea which
is so amorous after me,
That it is safe to allow it to lick my naked body all over with its tongues,
That it will not endanger me with the fevers that have deposited
themselves in it,
That all is clean forever and forever,
That the cool drink from the well tastes so good,
That blackberries are so flavorous and juicy,
That the fruits of the apple-orchard and the orange-orchard, that
melons, grapes, peaches, plums, will none of them poison me,
That when I recline on the grass I do not catch any disease,
Though probably every spear of grass rises out of what was once
catching disease.

text:
“For, when our souls have learnt to bear the fire,
The cloud shall fade, and we shall hear His voice,
Saying, “Come forth from out the grove, my dear desire,
And round my golden tent, like lambs, rejoice.”’
source_text:
‘For, when our souls have learned the heat to bear,
The cloud will vanish, we shall hear His voice,
Saying, “Come out from the grove, my love and care,
And round my golden tent like lambs rejoice.”’
